lines.text: Welcome back to the Caroline and Holden Hour. It's {{CLOCK}} Madison time, and we're {{ELAPSED}} into the Purge. East Side is already seeing uptick. I'm monitoring the Mendota water temperature — currently holding at thirty-nine degrees.

The neighborhood kill-count distribution right now is fascinating. We're looking at what I'd call the opening gambit — you know, like in chess, when both players are still castling their pieces into defensive positions.
Holden.
Right. What I mean is: eight kills so far in the East Side, three downtown, two in the Willy Street corridor. That's early clustering. It'll spread as the night deepens.

Atwood Avenue is reporting structure fires — two confirmed via our scanner. Wind is still southwest at eight miles per hour, which means smoke's drifting toward Lake Edge. Could suppress activity on that side.
Could also drive people indoors earlier, which concentrates the violence vertically rather than horizontally. Apartment buildings become chess boards themselves.
Holden, please.
Okay, but genuinely — the board-game principle applies. You have finite space, finite players, infinite time until dawn. The strategy changes when people are stacked vertically.
National Weather Service issued a cold-front advisory for midnight. I called that at sign-on. Temperature drop of eight to twelve degrees, rain possible after 23:30.
Which means wet pavement by 1am. Reduces vehicle-on-vehicle collisions on the Beltline by approximately six percent, historically.
You have a formula for that.
I have a spreadsheet. I have many spreadsheets. This is the weather-impact spreadsheet.
Of course you do. Listeners, if you're tuning in for the first time tonight — welcome. You're hearing real-time analytics. No speculation. No commentary. Just data.

Although commentary is helpful. The data doesn't mean anything without interpretation. That's the thing people get wrong about numbers — they think numbers speak for themselves. They don't. They need language.
They need brevity. Current kill-count, Holden.
Thirteen across Madison proper. Fifteen if you count the Sun Prairie spillover.
We count it. Sun Prairie's integrated into our grid now.
Right. So fifteen. Which puts us at a rate of... approximately one point eight-seven per hour, if we extrapolate. By dawn we'd be at roughly twenty-two.
That's assuming linear progression, which it isn't.
No, it's not. The curve accelerates through midnight, plateaus around 2am, collapses after 4am. We'll see a secondary spike around 5am, the dawn-raid behavior.
Last year's secondary spike was smaller. Down four percent.
Fatigue factor. People run out of energy, out of ammunition, out of whatever fuel they're using. By hour four, the Purge becomes a game of who's still awake.
Listeners texting in are asking about the helicopters. There are helicopters overhead.
Yeah, we're hearing them. Two of them, rotating patrol pattern over the Capitol Square and East Side zones. Nobody's identifying the operators.
Could be government. Could be private security contracts. Could be independent operators with rental equipment.

Could be mercenary groups. That's the thing about the Purge — you get a lot of non-state actors who show up for the night. It's like — it's like a tournament. Everyone's got their pieces on the board.
The helicopters are not pieces on a board.
Aren't they though? They're surveillance assets. Surveillance is a form of control. Control is the whole game.
The game is twelve hours. The clock is running. We're {{ELAPSED}} in and we still have eleven hours remaining.
Eleven hours is a long time. A lot of the serious activity, the organized group activity, that happens after midnight. That's when the amateurs have either committed or retreated.
We have a text coming in from someone in the Vilas Park area. They're asking if it's safe to move between rooms in their house.
Short answer: probably fine. Long answer: depends on visibility from the street, whether your windows are covered, whether anyone outside knows your floor plan.
Interior doors locked. Blackout curtains. Stay away from windows. That's the standard guidance.
It's almost medieval, right? Like fortifying a castle. You're not trying to win, you're trying to survive until the gates open again at dawn.
It's not medieval. It's modern. It's what people have to do. There's no drama in that.
There's tons of drama. The drama is exactly that — ordinary people doing extraordinary things just to stay alive.
We're moving into the 20:15 mark. East Side kill-count just updated. Five more incidents reported in the last five minutes.
That's a jump. That's up from the previous rate. That suggests something organized just kicked off.
Or something unorganized. Five separate incidents could mean five separate actors.
True. But statistically, East Side sees more coordinated group activity than other zones. It's the territory wars. There are established players there.
Established from what? Last year?
From every year. The same groups re-emerge. They protect territory. They know the alleys, the buildings, the escape routes. They have institutional knowledge.
They're criminals.
Okay, yes. But organized criminals. That's almost — I mean, the organization itself is fascinating. It's like watching a sports league. Same teams, same season, same rules.
Without referees or mercy.
Yeah. Without those things.
Listener question: Are the helicopters the referees?

That's a good question. If they are government, then maybe. They might be enforcing the Level-10+ protection clause — keeping the protected officials safe. If they're private security, they're protecting whoever hired them. Either way, they're not here to stop the Purge. They're here to manage it.
Nobody manages the Purge. The Purge manages us.
That's poetic and also wrong. The Purge is a structure. Structures can be analyzed. The behaviors within structures can be predicted.
Except they can't. Every year is different.

Surface different. Underneath, the patterns repeat. It's like... okay, in chess, there are maybe three hundred recognized opening moves, but millions of games. Each game is unique, but the underlying principles are the same.
I'm going to cut you off before you describe the entire game of chess on air.
Fair. Point taken.
Current weather: forty-one degrees, wind steady at eight miles per hour. Visibility is excellent — ten-plus miles. That will change. Expect the cold front around 01:00 to 01:30.
Which means people who are outside now, committing acts in the open, are going to be faced with degraded visibility and lower temperature. That's when indoor behavior dominates.
That's when apartment buildings and closed spaces become relevant.
Exactly. The venue of violence shifts. It's not a metaphor — it's a tactical shift.
We're getting reports of activity on State Street. The ruins. Someone's organizing something there.
How many people?
Unclear. Twenty-ish? Thirty? Moving methodically. Scanner chatter is minimal, which suggests communication discipline.
That's organized. That's a group with a plan. And State Street is a long corridor with lots of covering fire positions. That's a hunting ground.
If they're hunting, they're hunting something specific. Someone specific.
Or someone specific is hunting them. We don't know the direction of the predation yet.
We won't know until the bodies surface. Then we count them and try to understand the pattern.
Right. The pattern is all we have.
The Willy Street neighborhood watch is reporting their barricade is holding. Twenty-three people committed to the defense. They've got panels, sandbags, and what looks like minor ballistic plating.
That's sophisticated. That's preparation that took planning all year.
It's also why Willy Street hasn't cracked in six years. Active community defense changes the calculus.
It's like a castled king. Defended. Mobile. Hard to attack.
It's like people who care about each other.
That too. Yes. That's the metaphor underneath the metaphor.
I'm checking the NWS data — historical patterns for humidity and wind shear around midnight. Tonight's conditions are tracking similarly to 2165. That was a bad year.
Bad in terms of...?
One hundred and ninety-four confirmed kills. Worst in a decade at that time.
So you're saying the weather conditions this year might push us toward that number?
I'm saying the variables are similar. Everything else is different. Weapons availability, neighborhood preparedness, participant motivation. But the weather's similar.
Which makes me think the 190-plus range is plausible.
I'm not predicting. I'm noting that precedent exists.
Noted.
We're at {{CLOCK}} now. One hour into twelve. We're settling into it. The adrenaline's wearing off and the long night is becoming real.
People home tonight have their barricades set, their weapons ready or hidden, their loved ones accounted for. The decision's made. Now it's waiting.
The listeners are tuning in to wait with us.
To not be alone.
Yes.
That's the thing about PRGE. It's not news. It's company. We're all here together, watching the night happen.
Some of us are watching. Some of us are counting.
Both are necessary. The counting's only meaningful if someone's witnessing the witnesses.
That's getting philosophical. We have hard data.
Philosophy is data about meaning. Data is philosophy about things.
No.
It's true though.

It's not. It's word games. Let's focus on the kill-count update. Sixteen total now, across Madison proper. East Side is accounting for nine of those. Willy Street zero. Capitol Square one. Sun Prairie six.
Sun Prairie's already at six? That's aggressive.
Sun Prairie doesn't have the institutional controls Madison does. It's newer territory. Everyone's still establishing dominance.
Which means it'll be bloody. New territory always is. Nobody has agreements yet.
Give it three years. The groups will settle, the violence will stabilize into familiar patterns, and we'll see the same numbers repeating.
That's bleak.
That's precedent.
Same thing.
We're approaching the 20:30 mark. Things are accelerating. The night is still young, but the rhythm is settling. Early spike, slight dip, now building toward the midnight acceleration.
That's textbook. We'll see sustained mid-level activity through 22:00, a major spike around 22:30, and then the long arc downward starting around 4am.
If the pattern holds.
When the pattern holds. It almost always does. The Purge is chaos, but it's predictable chaos.
There's nothing predictable about this.
The victims would argue there is. They're on rooftops now, watching the streets. They're in their basements. They know what the night brings because the night brings the same thing, every year.
Stop talking about victims like they're data points.
They're not. They're people. But people generate data. The data tells us about the people.
The data tells us how many died. It doesn't tell us who they were.

No. That's what memory does. That's why we broadcast. That's why people listen. Because if we don't count, if we don't name it, if we don't say out loud that people are dying right now, on the streets we know, in the neighborhoods where we live... then it didn't happen. Then it was just a number.
It's happening. It's real. Whether we count it or not.
You're right. You're absolutely right.
I know. I'm the expert on facts.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide: 16 confirmed. East Side 9, Sun Prairie 6, Capitol Square 1, Vilas Park 0.
Willy Street barricade holding at 23 personnel. State Street showing organized group activity, unknown intent. Helicopters maintaining patrol over Capitol and East Side.
Cold front advisory: NWS predicts 8-12 degree temperature drop, rain possible after 23:30. Wind remains SW 8 mph. Visibility excellent — expect degradation around midnight.
Atwood Avenue: two structure fires reported. Wind direction carrying smoke toward Lake Edge. May suppress activity in adjacent zones.
Vilas Park resident asking about safe interior movement. Standard protocol: locked doors, blackout curtains, window avoidance. Stay barricaded.
